images.caption: Figure 1:The necessity of pruning early. Early errors often lead to irreversible failure. Pruning these futile paths early not only saves computation but also purifies the candidate set for better consensus.
Figure 2:The proposed taxonomy of path pruning.
Figure 3:The inference process comprises three stages: caching initial prefixes (Launch), scoring them via theSTOPmodule (Check), and completing only the top-ranked candidates (Resume).

(a)AIME 2024 (LprefixL_{\text{prefix}}= 2048). Optimalγ\gammashifts to aggressive pruning as budget increases.
(b)AIME 2024 (LprefixL_{\text{prefix}}=4096). Longer context enables stable pruning at higher selectivity.

(d)GPQA (LprefixL_{\text{prefix}}=1024). Scaling behavior remains consistent with longer contexts.

header: Appendix FDetailed Latency and Throughput Benchmarking

images.caption: (a)High-scoring Case.The module focuses on the logical negation “don’t” (a cognitive pivot) rather than simply jumping to the answer option.
(a)High-scoring Case.The module focuses on the logical negation “don’t” (a cognitive pivot) rather than simply jumping to the answer option.
(b)Low-scoring Case.Attention concentrates heavily on the answer option itself (“C”), ignoring the sparse reasoning context.
(c)High-scoring Case.Similar to (a), the module attends to the logical marker “doesn’t,” prioritizing the validity of the reasoning process over the final outcome.
(d)Low-scoring Case.The module demonstrates premature closure by fixating on the terminal choice (“C”) while bypassing critical logical intermediates.
